news_summary: Recent environmental developments highlight nature's resilience with fungi aiding wildfire recovery and water and wildlife returning to farmland ponds, while global efforts increasingly focus on renewable energy transitions despite challenges in biochar implementation.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes évolutions environnementales mettent en évidence la résilience de la nature : les champignons contribuent à la régénération des zones touchées par les incendies, tandis que l'eau et la faune reviennent dans les étangs des terres agricoles ; parallèlement, les efforts mondiaux se concentrent de plus en plus sur la transition vers les énergies renouvelables, malgré les difficultés rencontrées dans la mise en œuvre du biochar.
news_summary_es: Los últimos acontecimientos medioambientales ponen de relieve la resiliencia de la naturaleza: los hongos contribuyen a la recuperación tras los incendios forestales y el agua y la fauna silvestre regresan a los estanques de las tierras de cultivo, mientras que los esfuerzos a nivel mundial se centran cada vez más en la transición hacia las energías renovables, a pesar de los retos que plantea la implementación del biocarbón.

articles.title: Are fire-loving fungi mother nature’s first responders after wildfires?
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM After a severe wildfire, the first visible signs of life returning are often flowers or birds. What you cannot see is what arrives even earlier. Within weeks of a blaze, tiny fungal fruiting bodies push through scorched soil and release spores, briefly carpeting otherwise-bare ground in splashes of ocher, […]
The post Are fire-loving fungi mother nature’s first responders after wildfires? first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

After a severe wildfire, the first visible signs of life returning are often flowers or birds. What you cannot see is what arrives even earlier.

Within weeks of a blaze, tiny fungal fruiting bodies push through scorched soil and release spores, briefly carpeting otherwise-bare ground in splashes of ocher, mauve, pink, and orange. These are pyrophilous fungi, from the Greek for “fire-loving,” and researchers are beginning to understand what they are actually doing, and why it matters for everything that comes after.

“They really seem like the first responders,” says Monika Fischer, a mycologist at the University of British Columbia. “They’re the things that are growing the most rapidly first.”

What a severe wildfire does to soil

High-intensity wildfires can reach temperatures over 1,000 degrees Celsius (1,832 degrees Fahrenheit). At those temperatures, the top layer of soil and any organisms living in it essentially burn away, leaving behind what scientists call pyrolyzed organic material, a mix of soot and charcoal that is almost entirely carbon.

The problem is that the carbon atoms in this material form fused rings, creating dense, complicated clumps. “Very few microbes, or very few organisms, could just take that in to try to eat it,” says Matt Traxler, a microbiologist at the University of California, Berkeley. Pyrolyzed material also contains toxic compounds called polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons.

On top of that, burned soil develops a water-resistant, waxy coating that prevents moisture from seeping into the ground, leaving the land prone to landslides and making the soil dry and crumbly, making it difficult for seeds to take hold.

How fire fungi solve the problem

Pyrophilous fungi appear to have evolved specifically to function in this inhospitable terrain. In laboratory experiments, Fischer and Traxler showed that one well-studied post-fire species, Pyronema domesticum, can grow even when burnt soil is the only available carbon source. When placed in pyrolyzed material, the fungus switches on genes that produce enzymes to break down the charred matter, converting it into a more accessible form that other organisms can use.

“They’re sort of jump-starting that nutrient cycling,” Fischer says. A recent study from the University of California, Riverside, found that other pyrophilous species also carry the key genes for digesting charcoal.

Beyond the chemistry, fire fungi appear to help repair the physical structure of the soil. Following the 2016 wildfires in Great Smoky Mountains National Park, mycologist Karen Hughes of the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, and her collaborators found networks of mycelial mats across the burned landscape, tangled webs of fine, root-like structures that fungi use to absorb water and nutrients. Those mats help stabilize soil that has been destabilized by fire. Similar formations have been observed after wildfires in the Pacific Northwest and in southeastern Australia.

Why this research is gaining urgency

As wildfires grow more severe and more frequent, the role of fire fungi is getting more attention. Fischer believes these organisms, and the mycelial mats they build, likely play a significant part in helping seeds germinate after a fire. Traxler says scientists are still working out how early differences in fungal communities shape the pace and diversity of long-term recovery.

There is a great deal still to learn. With an estimated five million species of fungi on Earth, Hughes acknowledges there is “a ton of stuff we don’t know.” But the current research is building toward something practical. Fischer says a better understanding of fire fungi could give ecologists and land managers a toolkit to actively support post-fire regeneration: not just waiting for recovery to happen, but knowing which organisms to encourage and when.

The work of ecological recovery begins long before the first wildflower appears. It starts underground, in the microscopic chemistry of charcoal being broken apart by organisms most people have never heard of. Small, colorful cups pushing through ash are easy to overlook. But researchers are now making the case that the plants and animals that eventually return are only able to do so because the fungi arrived first and made the soil ready for them.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant ecological discovery about pyrophilous fungi that play a crucial role in post-wildfire soil recovery, nutrient cycling, and ecosystem regeneration. This has broad environmental implications given the increasing frequency and severity of wildfires globally. The research provides meaningful insights into natural processes that can aid large-scale land restoration and informs practical ecological management strategies.
articles.personality_title: Fire-loving fungi help nature recover after wildfires
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – After a wildfire, the land looks burnt and lifeless. The soil’s top layer is heated so much that it becomes covered in charcoal and toxic compounds. This makes it hard for plants to grow back quickly.

**What happened** – Scientists have discovered that special fungi, called pyrophilous fungi, are often the first living things to appear after a fire. These fungi grow through the burned soil within weeks, breaking down the charcoal and changing the soil so other plants and animals can return.

**Impact** – These fungi speed up the natural recovery of the land by turning tough, burnt material into nutrients. They also create networks in the soil that help hold water and keep the soil stable. This is important because it helps seeds grow and prevents problems like landslides.

**What’s next step** – Researchers want to learn more about how different fungi help the land heal. This knowledge could help people manage forests better after fires by encouraging the right fungi to grow, making the recovery faster and stronger.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Fire-loving fungi are nature’s early helpers, preparing burned soil so plants and animals can come back after wildfires.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les champignons amateurs de feu aident la nature à se rétablir après les incendies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Après un incendie, le sol semble brûlé et sans vie. La couche supérieure du sol est chauffée à un point tel qu’elle devient recouverte de charbon et de composés toxiques, ce qui rend difficile la croissance rapide des plantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert que des champignons spéciaux, appelés champignons pyrophiles, sont souvent les premiers êtres vivants à apparaître après un feu. Ces champignons poussent à travers le sol brûlé en quelques semaines, décomposant le charbon et transformant le sol pour que d’autres plantes et animaux puissent revenir.

**Impact** – Ces champignons accélèrent la récupération naturelle des terres en transformant les matières brûlées en nutriments. Ils créent aussi des réseaux dans le sol qui aident à retenir l’eau et à stabiliser le sol. Cela aide les graines à pousser et empêche des problèmes comme les glissements de terrain.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent mieux comprendre comment différents champignons aident la terre à guérir. Ces connaissances pourraient aider à mieux gérer les forêts après les incendies en favorisant la croissance des bons champignons, rendant la récupération plus rapide et plus forte.

**Phrase clé** – Les champignons amateurs de feu sont les premiers aides de la nature, préparant le sol brûlé pour que plantes et animaux puissent revenir après les incendies.
articles.personality_title_es: Los hongos amantes del fuego ayudan a la naturaleza a recuperarse tras los incendios
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Después de un incendio, el suelo parece quemado y sin vida. La capa superior del suelo se calienta tanto que queda cubierta de carbón y compuestos tóxicos, lo que dificulta que las plantas crezcan rápidamente.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos descubrieron que unos hongos especiales, llamados hongos pirófilos, son a menudo los primeros organismos vivos en aparecer tras un incendio. Estos hongos crecen a través del suelo quemado en pocas semanas, descomponen el carbón y transforman el suelo para que otras plantas y animales puedan volver.

**Impacto** – Estos hongos aceleran la recuperación natural del terreno al convertir el material quemado en nutrientes. También forman redes en el suelo que ayudan a retener agua y estabilizar el terreno. Esto facilita que las semillas crezcan y previene problemas como deslizamientos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren entender mejor cómo diferentes hongos ayudan a sanar la tierra. Este conocimiento podría ayudar a manejar mejor los bosques después de incendios, promoviendo el crecimiento de los hongos adecuados para acelerar y fortalecer la recuperación.

**Frase clave** – Los hongos amantes del fuego son los primeros ayudantes de la naturaleza, preparando el suelo quemado para que plantas y animales puedan regresar tras los incendios.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a gently charred forest floor just after a wildfire, where vibrant, small fungal fruiting bodies in ocher, mauve, pink, and orange emerge from dark, cracked soil textured with soot and charcoal; delicate, glowing mycelial networks subtly weave through the earth beneath, symbolizing the fungi’s vital role as nature’s first responders restoring life and nurturing the ground for future growth.

articles.title: Middle East crisis exposes global energy fault line as UN urges shift to renewables
articles.summary: The ongoing crisis in the Middle East is exposing a central vulnerability in the global economy: the dependence on fossil fuels flowing through regions affected by conflict, a situation which is strengthening the UN’s case for a faster transition to cheaper, more resilient renewable power.
articles.content:
The Hormuz Strait in the Persian Gulf, through which one fifth of the world’s supply of oil and gas passes, has been largely closed to shipping since the conflict involving Iran, the United States and Israel, as well as, to a lesser extent, other nations, began a month ago.

The disruption to supplies has already resulted in reduced access to the fossil fuels which countries around the world need to produce power, leading to higher prices rattling global markets in the process.

The United Nations says the bottleneck caused by the virtual closure of the strait underscores a fundamental issue that energy security is no longer just about supply, but also about resilience and finding alternative power sources in an increasingly unstable world.

Why it matters

Concern over the use of fossil fuels has typically been linked to climate change, due to the warming effects of the gases they produce when burned, but now energy security has come more into focus.

Earlier this year, the UN Secretary-General António Guterres cautioned that “in this age of war…our addiction to fossil fuels is destabilizing both the climate and global security.”

The impact of war

Since the outbreak of the war in the Middle East, it has become increasingly evident that:

Important oil and gas supplies are concentrated in regions vulnerable to conflict

Transport routes can be disrupted by military escalation

Price volatility quickly spreads across economies

The big picture

As countries around the world continue to rely on fossil fuels to meet the daily needs of citizens and to drive economic growth, it has become clear that they are more vulnerable than ever before to sudden supply disruptions.

Stable and strategic relations with other countries to ensure power supplies are at a premium as energy demands grow.

“Three-fourths of humanity lives in countries that are net importers of fossil fuels, dependent on energy they do not control, at prices they cannot predict,” said Mr. Guterres in February this year.

© World Bank Technicians work on a solar panel in Chattisgarh State, India.

The UN chief also warned about the risk of development budgets “siphoned into fuel bills, at the constant mercy of geopolitical turmoil and supply disruptions”, insisting: “We must stop treating the transition away from fossil fuels as taboo.”

The promise of renewable energy

One solution to guard against the crisis and chaos caused by a lack of access to fossil fuels is to transition to renewable energy sources, for example solar, wind and water power.

These offer a fundamentally different power provision model, one that is more widely accessible and potentially cheaper.

Renewable energy is often locally sourced, domestically produced, and thus less vulnerable to the global upheaval that geopolitical crises can spark.

As the UN’s climate chief Simon Stiell emphasized earlier this year, “renewables are the clearest, cheapest path to energy security and sovereignty, shielding countries and economies from shocks unleashed by wars, trade turmoil and the ‘might-is-right’ politics that leave every nation poorer.”

Energy transition underway

The transition from fossil fuel sources to renewable energy has already begun.

Kenya, in East Africa, has become a global leader in renewable energy, particularly geothermal power, generating the vast majority of its electricity from renewables.

The South American nation, Chile, is one of the world’s fastest-growing renewable energy markets. It has moved away from coal-fired power generation by scaling up solar and wind power, taking advantage of natural conditions of the type found in the Atacama Desert.

India, too, has focused on expanding solar and wind infrastructure, integrating renewables into its national development and electrification strategies, although it still relies heavily on oil and gas which are typically delivered through the Strait of Hormuz from countries bordering the Persian Gulf.

Read the UN’s 2025 Energy Progress Report

People’s power, the bottom line

While energy security is a growing geopolitical issue that sovereign nations need to address, it is also deeply personal, affecting families and individuals around the world, as people face higher energy bills and an overall increase in the cost of living.

Unsplash/Sippakorn Yamkasikorn Petrol prices are increasing globally.

In most cases, according to the UN, renewables are cheaper than coal, oil or gas, and so can directly lower electricity costs for households.

Renewables can also protect people from future price spikes by providing cheaper, more stable power directly to communities.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant global issue of energy insecurity exacerbated by geopolitical conflicts and presents the UN's strong advocacy for a transition to renewable energy. It details the broad positive impact of renewables on energy security, economic stability, and climate action, supported by concrete examples from multiple countries actively expanding renewable infrastructure. The story focuses on a single, substantial topic with enough context to demonstrate the tangible benefits of renewable energy adoption worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Middle East conflict highlights need for faster switch to renewable energy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Middle East crisis has caused major problems in global energy supplies. A key route for oil and gas, the Hormuz Strait, has been mostly closed for about a month due to conflict involving Iran, the United States, and Israel. This area normally carries one-fifth of the world’s oil and gas.

**What happened** – Because the strait is blocked, many countries are facing shortages of fossil fuels like oil and gas. This has pushed energy prices higher and shaken markets worldwide. The United Nations says this shows how relying on fossil fuels from unstable regions creates big risks for energy security.

**Impact** – This situation is important because it reveals that energy security isn’t just about having enough supply, but also about having power sources that can handle disruptions. The UN points out that three-quarters of the world’s people live in countries that depend on imported fossil fuels, which makes them vulnerable to conflicts and price changes. Renewable energy like solar and wind can be made locally and is less affected by global problems.

**What's next step** – Many countries are already shifting to renewable energy. Kenya uses mostly geothermal power, Chile is growing fast in solar and wind, and India is expanding its solar and wind projects. The UN encourages all countries to speed up this transition to avoid future energy crises and reduce costs for families.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Middle East conflict shows why the world needs to move quickly to renewable energy for safer, more affordable power that is less affected by wars and supply disruptions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le conflit au Moyen-Orient souligne l'urgence d'une transition vers les énergies renouvelables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La crise au Moyen-Orient a provoqué de graves problèmes dans l’approvisionnement énergétique mondial. Le détroit d’Ormuz, une voie clé pour le pétrole et le gaz, est presque fermé depuis un mois à cause d’un conflit impliquant l’Iran, les États-Unis et Israël. Cette zone transporte normalement un cinquième du pétrole et du gaz mondial.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En raison de la fermeture du détroit, de nombreux pays manquent de combustibles fossiles comme le pétrole et le gaz. Cela a fait augmenter les prix de l’énergie et perturbé les marchés mondiaux. L’ONU explique que cette situation montre les risques liés à la dépendance aux énergies fossiles provenant de régions instables.

**Impact** – Cette situation est importante car elle montre que la sécurité énergétique ne dépend pas seulement de la quantité disponible, mais aussi de la capacité à faire face aux interruptions. L’ONU souligne que trois quarts de la population mondiale vivent dans des pays importateurs de combustibles fossiles, ce qui les rend vulnérables aux conflits et aux variations de prix. Les énergies renouvelables comme le solaire et l’éolien sont souvent produites localement et moins touchées par les crises mondiales.

**Prochaine étape** – Plusieurs pays passent déjà aux énergies renouvelables. Le Kenya utilise principalement la géothermie, le Chili développe rapidement solaire et éolien, et l’Inde étend ses projets solaires et éoliens. L’ONU encourage tous les pays à accélérer cette transition pour éviter de futures crises énergétiques et réduire les coûts pour les familles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le conflit au Moyen-Orient montre pourquoi le monde doit rapidement adopter les énergies renouvelables pour une énergie plus sûre, moins chère et moins dépendante des guerres et des interruptions d’approvisionnement.
articles.personality_title_es: El conflicto en Oriente Medio destaca la necesidad de acelerar el cambio a energías renovables
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La crisis en Oriente Medio ha causado grandes problemas en el suministro mundial de energía. El estrecho de Ormuz, una ruta clave para el petróleo y gas, ha estado casi cerrado durante un mes debido a un conflicto entre Irán, Estados Unidos e Israel. Esta zona normalmente transporta una quinta parte del petróleo y gas mundial.

**Qué pasó** – Debido al bloqueo del estrecho, muchos países enfrentan escasez de combustibles fósiles como petróleo y gas. Esto ha aumentado los precios de la energía y ha afectado los mercados globales. La ONU dice que esto muestra los riesgos de depender de combustibles fósiles de regiones inestables.

**Impacto** – Esta situación es importante porque revela que la seguridad energética no solo depende de tener suficiente suministro, sino también de contar con fuentes de energía que puedan resistir interrupciones. La ONU señala que tres cuartas partes de la población mundial viven en países que importan combustibles fósiles, haciéndolos vulnerables a conflictos y cambios de precios. Las energías renovables como la solar y eólica se producen localmente y son menos afectadas por problemas globales.

**Próximo paso** – Muchos países ya están cambiando a energías renovables. Kenia usa principalmente energía geotérmica, Chile está creciendo rápidamente en solar y eólica, e India está ampliando sus proyectos solares y eólicos. La ONU anima a todos los países a acelerar esta transición para evitar futuras crisis energéticas y reducir costos para las familias.

**Resumen en una frase** – El conflicto en Oriente Medio muestra por qué el mundo necesita avanzar rápido hacia las energías renovables para tener energía más segura, más barata y menos afectada por guerras y cortes en el suministro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast, calm Persian Gulf with a symbolic closed shipping lane marked by gentle waves forming a subtle barrier, contrasted with vibrant solar panels and wind turbines rising steadily on distant shores, connected by soft golden light beams symbolizing energy transition and resilience.

articles.title: Water and wildlife return to farmland ponds
articles.summary: Two restored ponds on the Hertfordshire-Essex border see recovery, including breeding newts.
articles.content: The ponds on the Hertfordshire-Essex border, close to Clavering, have held water throughout summer for the first time in more than a decade.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the successful restoration of farmland ponds leading to water retention and wildlife recovery, including breeding newts. This represents a positive environmental impact with meaningful conservation benefits beyond a small niche, showing tangible ecosystem recovery.
articles.personality_title: Restored farmland ponds bring water and newts back to Hertfordshire-Essex border
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For over ten years, two ponds on farmland near Clavering, located on the border between Hertfordshire and Essex, often dried up during the summer months. This made it hard for water and wildlife to survive there.

**What happened** – Recently, these two ponds were restored, and for the first time in more than a decade, they have held water all summer long. As a result, wildlife has started to return, including newts that are now breeding in the ponds.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that restoring small natural habitats on farmland can help bring back animals and plants that had disappeared. The breeding newts are a clear sign that the ecosystem is improving, which helps the environment in a real and measurable way.

**What’s next step** – The success of these ponds could encourage more restoration projects in other farmland areas. Scientists and conservationists will likely continue monitoring the ponds to see how wildlife develops and possibly expand efforts to protect more habitats.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Restoring farmland ponds on the Hertfordshire-Essex border has successfully brought back water and breeding newts, showing how small changes can help nature recover.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les étangs restaurés des terres agricoles ramènent l'eau et les tritons à la frontière Hertfordshire-Essex
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant plus de dix ans, deux étangs situés dans des terres agricoles près de Clavering, à la frontière entre Hertfordshire et Essex, s'asséchaient souvent en été. Cela rendait difficile la survie de l'eau et des animaux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, ces deux étangs ont été restaurés et, pour la première fois depuis plus d'une décennie, ils ont conservé de l'eau tout l'été. En conséquence, la faune a commencé à revenir, y compris des tritons qui se reproduisent maintenant dans les étangs.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre que restaurer de petits habitats naturels dans les terres agricoles peut aider à faire revenir des animaux et des plantes disparus. La reproduction des tritons est un signe clair que l'écosystème s'améliore, ce qui aide l'environnement de manière concrète.

**Prochaine étape** – Le succès de ces étangs pourrait encourager d'autres projets de restauration dans d'autres zones agricoles. Les scientifiques et les conservateurs vont probablement continuer à surveiller les étangs pour voir comment la faune évolue et peut-être étendre les efforts pour protéger plus d'habitats.

**Message essentiel en une phrase** – La restauration des étangs agricoles à la frontière Hertfordshire-Essex a permis de ramener de l'eau et des tritons en reproduction, montrant comment de petits changements peuvent aider la nature à se rétablir.
articles.personality_title_es: Estanques restaurados en tierras agrícolas devuelven agua y tritones a la frontera Hertfordshire-Essex
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante más de diez años, dos estanques en tierras agrícolas cerca de Clavering, en la frontera entre Hertfordshire y Essex, se secaban a menudo durante el verano. Esto dificultaba que el agua y la vida silvestre sobrevivieran allí.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, estos dos estanques fueron restaurados y, por primera vez en más de una década, han mantenido agua durante todo el verano. Como resultado, la vida silvestre ha comenzado a regresar, incluidos tritones que ahora se están reproduciendo en los estanques.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que restaurar pequeños hábitats naturales en tierras agrícolas puede ayudar a que animales y plantas que habían desaparecido regresen. La reproducción de los tritones es una señal clara de que el ecosistema está mejorando, lo que ayuda al medio ambiente de manera real y medible.

**Próximo paso** – El éxito de estos estanques podría animar a realizar más proyectos de restauración en otras áreas agrícolas. Los científicos y conservacionistas probablemente seguirán monitoreando los estanques para ver cómo se desarrolla la vida silvestre y posiblemente ampliar los esfuerzos para proteger más hábitats.

**Mensaje en una frase** – La restauración de estanques en tierras agrícolas en la frontera Hertfordshire-Essex ha logrado devolver el agua y los tritones en reproducción, demostrando cómo pequeños cambios pueden ayudar a la naturaleza a recuperarse.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of serene farmland ponds on the Hertfordshire-Essex border, with clear water reflecting the soft sky, surrounded by lush grasses and reeds, where vibrant frogs and dragonflies gather peacefully, symbolizing the return of water and thriving wildlife after a long dry spell.

articles.title: Biochar Was a Billion-Ton Dream, the Reality Is More Complicated
articles.summary:
Picture turning yard waste, wood scraps, and farm leftovers into something that stores carbon underground...
The post Biochar Was a Billion-Ton Dream, the Reality Is More Complicated appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Picture turning yard waste, wood scraps, and farm leftovers into something that stores carbon underground for centuries and improves soil health. That’s the idea behind biochar. While this is true, it doesn’t tell the full story.

For over twenty years, researchers, entrepreneurs, and climate advocates have promoted biochar as a top way to remove carbon dioxide from the air. Early estimates said it could take out 3.4 to 6.3 billion tons of CO₂ each year, which is huge. This excitement led to many scientific papers, startup investments, and carbon credit deals.

But a new analysis in Nature Sustainability from January 2026 says we should slow down. Biochar is real, but the excitement has gotten ahead of the facts. The researchers warn that too much hype could lead to a “boom-and-bust cycle” that ends up hurting the technology.

What Is Biochar?

Biochar is charcoal, but not the kind you use at a backyard cookout. It’s made by heating organic materials such as wood chips, crop waste, or agricultural byproducts in a low-oxygen environment through a process called pyrolysis. The result is a dark, porous, carbon-rich material that resists breaking down in soil for centuries or even millennia.

The inspiration came from an unlikely source: ancient Amazonian soils. Researchers discovered that the region’s famously fertile “terra preta” (Portuguese for “dark earth”) owed its richness to charcoal that Indigenous peoples had mixed into the soil thousands of years ago. That charcoal had survived intact, still improving soil structure and fertility long after the civilization that made it passed into history.

When scientists studied terra preta, they realized that locking carbon in a solid form and burying it in soil removes it from the air for a long time. Biochar looked like a win-win: it could store carbon and help farms. This led to more funding, research, and new companies.

The Numbers That Raised Alarms

The issue isn’t that biochar doesn’t work, but it hasn’t lived up to the early high hopes. The Nature Sustainability analysis by Italian soil scientists Luciano Gristina and Riccardo Scalenghe explains the numbers in detail.

Let’s look at production. All certified biochar facilities in the world make about 350,000 tons each year. That might sound like a lot, but spread over the world’s 1.5 billion hectares of farmland, it’s tiny. The researchers found that this would raise the soil surface by less than one-tenth the width of a human hair per year. This shows how far current production is from what’s needed for climate goals.

Next is the question of carbon storage. Biochar’s actual impact is about a thousand times smaller than early estimates. Even after subtracting the emissions from making it, the net climate benefit is only a few hundred thousand tons of CO₂ at most. For comparison, global emissions are about 36 billion tons each year.

Economics make things even harder. Studies show that feedstock—the raw material for biochar—can make up as much as 75% of the total cost. So, biochar projects only make financial sense if they have free or very cheap biomass, or steady income from carbon credits. Without these, most projects aren’t profitable.

In Southeast Asia, trials showed that adding biochar to farmland produced only modest yield improvements, not nearly enough to justify the cost for smallholder farmers without a subsidy.

Too Many Papers, Not Enough Proof

The researchers have another worry: there is so much research on biochar now that it looks like a bubble.

Scientific papers on biochar have jumped from fewer than 10 a year in the early 2000s to over 1,000 a year by the 2020s. The researchers point out that biochar now gets much more attention than older topics like acid rain, which was a major environmental issue studied for decades.

Much of this increase in papers comes from a small group of very active authors. A 2023 report in Nature found that the number of scientists publishing over 60 papers a year—more than one per week—has almost quadrupled in less than ten years. Biochar is a clear example, with a few names dominating the field and shaping how mature it seems.

There are now warning signs from institutions. According to Clarivate’s Web of Science index, two major journals that published a lot of biochar research, Chemosphere and Science of the Total Environment, were removed from the index for not meeting editorial standards. Investigations found problems like peer-review manipulation, fake reviewer identities, and unusual authorship practices. This shows that the scientific community is starting to push back on a field that may be moving too quickly for the evidence.

The worry isn’t that biochar researchers are being dishonest. It’s that career incentives reward publishing quickly rather than publishing carefully. Field experiments are slow and expensive. Lab results are faster. When the pressure to publish outpaces the ability to verify, fields can develop an inflated sense of their own progress, and then crash when reality catches up. Biochar has value, but it must be scaled to the right size to make environmental and economic sense.

What Would an Effective Biochar Path Look Like?

The Nature Sustainability report doesn’t say biochar is a lost cause. Instead, it suggests the field needs a reset: fewer papers, more checking; less speed, more solid research.

Specifically, the researchers call for:

Pre-registered trial designs so that results can’t be cherry-picked after the fact

Open data and public protocols that allow independent researchers to check each other’s work

Dedicated “verification articles” that reproduce influential findings before new claims pile on top of them

Funding earmarked for confirmatory studies and even negative results — research that shows what doesn’t work, not just what does

Evaluation metrics that reward verified contributions over sheer publication counts

The acid rain parallel is instructive. In the 1980s, acid rain was a front-page environmental crisis, the subject of intense scientific and policy debate. It receded from headlines not because the problem was imaginary, but because coordinated policy — cleaner fuels, emissions standards, pollution controls — actually reduced sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxide emissions. Evidence of ecosystem recovery followed. The field moved from alarm to action to outcome, a model worth following.

For biochar, the right approach is to be honest about what it can and can’t do. More real-world projects are now working within these limits.

Five Biochar Projects To Watch

Even with big challenges, some biochar projects around the world are finding success. They usually use local waste materials and earn money from more than just carbon credits.

Exomad Green — Bolivia

Exomad Green is currently the world’s largest biochar producer, operating two facilities that together remove about 260,000 tons of CO₂ per year. The feedstock is sawmill waste, wood residues that would otherwise be open-burned. The material is converted into biochar through pyrolysis, in other words, it is burned. That biochar is then donated to indigenous farming communities to improve degraded soils. In May 2025, Microsoft signed a 10-year agreement with Exomad Green for 1.24 million tons of CO₂ removal; the largest single biochar deal ever made. The model works because the feedstock is genuinely waste material with no better use, and the soil co-benefits for local communities are real and documented.

Pacific Biochar — California, USA

Pacific Biochar has built its model around a genuine dual benefit: it collects organic material from forests with high wildfire risk, reducing the fuel load that makes fires catastrophic, and converts that material into biochar for agricultural use. In 2024, CDR.fyi recognized Pacific Biochar as the global leader in durable carbon removal deliveries, accounting for 21% of total global certified volume. The California focus matters: the state’s wildfire crisis creates a near-endless supply of biomass that genuinely needs to be removed from the landscape, making the feedstock economics unusually solid.

Novocarbo — Germany

Novocarbo represents a different economic logic: the “Carbon Removal Park” model, where biochar production is bundled with renewable energy generation. At its flagship facility in Grevesmühlen, Germany, plant residues are converted into biochar using advanced pyrolysis units, and the waste heat from that process — about 6,600 megawatt-hours per year — is piped to roughly 1,800 nearby households for heating. Carbon credits are one revenue stream; district heating fees are another. That diversification makes the project less dependent on voluntary carbon market prices, which can be volatile. Novocarbo secured €27 million in new funding in 2025 to expand the model across Europe.

Aperam BioEnergia — Brazil

Aperam BioEnergia, certified by Puro.earth, is one of the most established biochar projects in the Global South. Operating in Minas Gerais, Brazil, it converts forestry residues into biochar, with plans to produce 30,000 tons annually by 2026. The project has sold more than 100,000 tons of carbon removal credits since 2021 and supports sustainable forest management practices alongside its production. It’s a model that pairs industrial scale with regional feedstock — the biomass inputs are produced nearby, keeping transport emissions low.

Carbonity / Airex Energy — Québec, Canada

Airex Energy’s pyrolysis technology is the backbone of Carbonity’s new facility in Port-Cartier, Québec — slated to become the largest biochar plant in North America. The project, backed by a consortium including Groupe Rémabec and SUEZ, represents roughly CAD 80 million in investment and aims to produce 10,000 tons of biochar in 2025, scaling to 30,000 by 2026. The feedstock is forest residues from the surrounding region. Microsoft has already purchased 36,000 carbon credits from an associated supply deal. The project is notable for its scale, but also carries the scrutiny that comes with large industrial operations in sensitive northern ecosystems.

Local, Small, and Real

These five projects have something important in common. The strongest ones, both economically and environmentally, use waste materials, work close to where those materials come from to cut transport emissions, and find value beyond just selling carbon credits.

That’s the conclusion the Nature Sustainability researchers point toward, even if they don’t say it quite so directly. The biochar projects most likely to survive and do genuine good are the ones that would still make sense even if the voluntary carbon market collapsed tomorrow, because their feedstock is free or nearly free, their soil benefits are real and local, and their energy co-products create additional value.

What likely won’t work is the dream of scaling biochar fast and wide enough to make a big dent in the 36 billion tons of CO₂ released each year. The numbers just don’t add up—not now, and maybe not ever—unless there are big changes in cost, feedstock supply, and how quickly the science can be checked.

That doesn’t mean we should give up on biochar. Instead, we should be clear about what it is: a useful, long-lasting, local way to turn waste into something valuable, with real benefits for farmers and soil, and a real—if small—role in removing carbon. Not everything has to save the world to be worthwhile.

The lesson from the acid rain research and responses fits here too: the goal isn’t to keep chasing new research. It’s to let the evidence catch up, support projects that stand up to close review, and build something lasting. The way forward will include many smaller, local biochar initiatives, not monolithic, world-saving programs that over-promise, threatening a valid carbon sequestration strategy.

What You Can Do

Support verified projects. If you or your organization purchases carbon offsets, look for biochar credits certified by Puro.earth or Verra with transparent feedstock sourcing and publicly available lifecycle data.

If you or your organization purchases carbon offsets, look for biochar credits certified by Puro.earth or Verra with transparent feedstock sourcing and publicly available lifecycle data. Ask about feedstock. Not all biochar is created equal. Biochar made from waste materials that would otherwise be burned or decompose has much stronger climate credentials than biochar produced from purpose-grown crops.

Not all biochar is created equal. Biochar made from waste materials that would otherwise be burned or decompose has much stronger climate credentials than biochar produced from purpose-grown crops. Look for local applications. Some municipalities and agricultural extension programs are exploring biochar for compost enhancement and soil remediation. Local applications with local feedstocks are the most ecologically sound.

Some municipalities and agricultural extension programs are exploring biochar for compost enhancement and soil remediation. Local applications with local feedstocks are the most ecologically sound. Be skeptical of big numbers. If a company or project claims to sequester millions of tons of CO₂ per year through biochar alone, ask to see the verified delivery data — not just projections.

If a company or project claims to sequester millions of tons of CO₂ per year through biochar alone, ask to see the verified delivery data — not just projections. Follow the science, not the hype. The International Biochar Initiative maintains a more grounded overview of the field’s actual state of knowledge.
articles.reasoning: The article provides a nuanced and realistic assessment of biochar as a carbon removal and soil improvement technology. While it tempers early over-optimism, it highlights ongoing, verified projects that deliver tangible environmental benefits, such as carbon sequestration, wildfire risk reduction, and sustainable agriculture. It calls for more rigorous science and sustainable scaling, which is constructive for climate action. The story focuses on a significant environmental topic with broad societal relevance and offers detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: Biochar’s promise grows with careful science and local projects
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Biochar is a charcoal-like material made by heating plant waste without much oxygen. It stores carbon underground for a long time and can improve soil. Scientists once hoped it could remove billions of tons of carbon dioxide from the air each year, helping fight climate change.

**What happened** – A new study shows that biochar’s impact is smaller than first thought. Current biochar production is tiny compared to the land area it could cover, and the real amount of carbon it can store is much less than earlier estimates. Some scientific research on biochar grew too fast, leading to doubts about quality. But several real-world projects in Bolivia, California, Germany, Brazil, and Canada are successfully turning local plant waste into biochar, helping farmers and reducing wildfire risks.

**Impact** – This shows biochar is useful but not a quick fix for climate change. Its strength lies in local, carefully managed projects that use waste materials and offer benefits beyond carbon storage, like better soil and less fire fuel. The new approach calls for slower, more careful research and honest reporting, avoiding promises that are too big to keep.

**What's next step** – Scientists and companies will focus on verifying results and running projects that make economic sense without relying only on carbon credits. More transparent research and local efforts will guide biochar’s future. People who buy carbon offsets are encouraged to choose verified biochar projects using waste materials.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Biochar works best as a local, well-checked method to turn plant waste into soil help and carbon storage, rather than a giant climate solution on its own.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le biochar trouve sa place grâce à la science rigoureuse et aux projets locaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le biochar est un matériau semblable au charbon obtenu en chauffant des déchets végétaux avec peu d’oxygène. Il stocke le carbone sous terre pendant longtemps et améliore la qualité des sols. Les scientifiques espéraient qu’il pourrait éliminer des milliards de tonnes de dioxyde de carbone chaque année pour lutter contre le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude montre que l’impact réel du biochar est plus faible que prévu. La production actuelle est très faible par rapport à la surface agricole mondiale, et la quantité de carbone stockée est bien moindre que les premières estimations. La recherche scientifique a parfois progressé trop vite, ce qui a créé des doutes sur sa qualité. Pourtant, plusieurs projets concrets en Bolivie, Californie, Allemagne, Brésil et Canada réussissent à transformer les déchets locaux en biochar, aidant les agriculteurs et réduisant les risques d’incendie.

**Impact** – Cela prouve que le biochar est utile, mais pas une solution miracle contre le changement climatique. Sa force réside dans des projets locaux bien gérés utilisant des déchets, avec des bénéfices au-delà du stockage de carbone, comme l’amélioration des sols et la réduction des combustibles pour les feux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques et entreprises vont se concentrer sur la vérification des résultats et sur des projets économiquement viables, sans dépendre uniquement des crédits carbone. Des recherches plus transparentes et des efforts locaux guideront l’avenir du biochar. Les acheteurs de crédits carbone sont invités à choisir des projets certifiés utilisant des déchets.

**En une phrase** – Le biochar est une méthode locale et vérifiée pour transformer les déchets végétaux en amélioration des sols et stockage de carbone, plutôt qu’une solution climatique massive.
articles.personality_title_es: El biochar avanza con ciencia cuidadosa y proyectos locales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El biochar es un material parecido al carbón que se obtiene calentando desechos vegetales con poco oxígeno. Almacena carbono bajo tierra por mucho tiempo y mejora el suelo. Los científicos esperaban que pudiera eliminar miles de millones de toneladas de dióxido de carbono al año para combatir el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio muestra que el impacto real del biochar es menor de lo pensado. La producción actual es pequeña comparada con la superficie agrícola mundial, y la cantidad de carbono almacenada es mucho menor que las primeras estimaciones. La investigación científica creció demasiado rápido, generando dudas sobre su calidad. Sin embargo, varios proyectos reales en Bolivia, California, Alemania, Brasil y Canadá están convirtiendo residuos locales en biochar, ayudando a agricultores y reduciendo riesgos de incendios.

**Impacto** – Esto demuestra que el biochar es útil, pero no una solución rápida para el cambio climático. Su fortaleza está en proyectos locales bien gestionados que usan residuos y ofrecen beneficios más allá del almacenamiento de carbono, como mejorar el suelo y reducir combustible para incendios.

**Próximo paso** – Científicos y empresas se enfocarán en verificar resultados y ejecutar proyectos que tengan sentido económico sin depender solo de créditos de carbono. La investigación más transparente y los esfuerzos locales guiarán el futuro del biochar. Se recomienda a compradores de compensaciones de carbono elegir proyectos verificados que usen residuos.

**Una frase clave** – El biochar funciona mejor como un método local y comprobado para convertir residuos vegetales en ayuda para el suelo y almacenamiento de carbono, no como una solución climática gigante.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, porous charcoal mound nestled within rich, dark soil on a small farm plot, surrounded by scattered wood scraps and crop residues gently transforming into biochar, with subtle, translucent layers beneath showing centuries-old charcoal enriching the earth, all rendered in soft natural browns, deep blacks, and muted greens, evoking a hopeful yet grounded connection between ancient wisdom and modern sustainable farming.
